data.title: This Is How I Shield My Retirement Portfolio With Covered Call ETFs
data.description:
Seeing the title of this article, some of you might probably think that I am seriously missing something about how the covered call ETFs work. Usually, whenever we have had larger drawdowns, most of these vehicles have followed the declining market. In addition, the fact that covered call ETFs tend to yield above 10% does not make the case for portfolio protection stronger. We could even make a case that it makes things even more suspicious.

I totally get it and there is certainly a fair amount of truth in such a thinking. For example, if we look at some of the more established covered call ETFs that have been operational for more than 5 years - e.g., the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) - we will see that all of them have lost notable value in pronounced market decline situations.

Let's take the most recent correction that we had back in early April, this year:

We will see the same pattern for other popular covered call ETF names as well: such as the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX), the Neos S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), etc.

It is important to understand that not all covered call ETFs are created equal (although, it might seem so by looking at the largest and most popular ones). And by the word "equal" I mean that the lion's share of covered call ETFs are effectively tied to either S&P 500 (SPY) or Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), which are AI and Mag-7 biased indices. So, if these two start to melt down, the chances are very high that standard covered call ETF investors would see their portfolio prices and income stream stumble as well.

Having said that, let me unpack the less explored world of covered call ETFs, which is where, in my view, durable (or retirement) income investors can access two things: 1) shelter from the AI-induced plunge and 2) stable 10%+ income streams.

Covered call ETF shield against AI bubble

I will first level set our understanding of covered call ETFs and their value creation mechanism. In this case, I will be talking about out-of-the-money covered call ETFs, which is where the majority of new ETF supply is concentrated and where ~90% of my covered call ETF portfolio is located.

In this context, I really like how the TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (TSPY) - one of my long positions - manager has visualized the essence:

It is clear that due to the capped upside that stems from the sold call options on a specific underlying that the ETF has chosen to track, investing in covered call ETFs are inherently inferior products, when the market is trending upwards.

We can see how this has worked out in practice (i.e., the index beating the covered call ETFs, which base their strategies on it):

Now, there are obviously some environments, which are quite favorable for covered call ETF products:

  1. When the market trades sideways, where collected call premiums should warrant alpha (but given how the market usually performs, I would say that completely flat scenario is only theoretical).
  2. When the market nosedives, where the collected call premiums should provide slight downside protection relative to fully open downside that is associated with any pure-play index move (but still, this is an unpleasant scenario and probably not acceptable for those, who want to keep the values stable).
  3. When the market is slightly rising or declining, where the covered call ETFs can both capture the upside and enjoy full benefits of option premiums that should secure alpha or neutralize the correction risks (sweet spot case).

In my view, given where we are in the market cycle (almost 3-year long bull market with average annual return of ~20%, which is significantly exceeds the long-term average of 10%), I consider the three aforementioned scenarios quite realistic.

Having said that, let me take a step back.

At the end of the day, what matters for me and I would say many other retirement (durable) income focused investors is not whether alpha is captured or not. Instead, the key thing is to secure durable passive income streams that are not funded by the underlying NAV or principal erosion.

And here there are two relevant risks that we have to appreciate as covered call ETF investors:

  1. Reduced income streams from compressed volatility levels.
  2. Steep correction after which the markets remain depressed for quite long period, which would ultimately shrink the underlying's asset base from which covered call ETFs could be written (i.e., also an income reduction case).

Now, regarding the first risk, I am not worried at all. The past couple of years we have lived in a relatively low vol environment in which covered call ETFs have still managed to deliver double digit yields. Furthermore, currently, we are not in an elevated volatility world, which would render the yields abnormally high and more likely subject to correction as the vol normalizes. The S&P VIX Index (VIX) chart below captures this nicely:

However, the second risk is where I would spend more time and try to understand how to navigate that.

The thing is that if any of the major indices like SPY or QQQ enter into free-fall and after hitting the bottom remain lying on the ground for a prolonged time period (at least more than 1 month, which is the usual period for OTM call options), then the chances would be very high that most covered call ETFs would experience unpleasant income reductions.

Here in this chart we can see how JEPI's dividends sharply dropped in early 2021 (the lagged effects kicked in from the COVID-19 driven sell-off). Then another drop happened in early/mid 2023, which stemmed from the 2022 bear market:

Granted, from all of the situations JEPI has managed to recover due to booming markets.

Yet, the question remains: what would happen if there was not a subsequent recovery (at least not as fast and extensive). The answer is that most probably these covered call ETF investors would have to live with significantly smaller dividends.

This is not a risk that I want to take.

The only way to shield the portfolio income streams is through introducing a better downside protection than what the overall market (in this case QQQ and SPY) has. The only way to get it in place is by selecting covered call ETFs, which are not linked to QQQ or SPY and embody some characteristics that might be beneficial in the case of AI drawdown.

The way how I am expressing this view is by going long a diversified blend of defensive covered call ETFs. For example, I have integrated the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) - exposure to physical gold - in my portfolio to access protection from rising fiscal deficit and damaged Fed independence driven sell-offs. I have bought the Neos Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) as well to get an exposure to real estate, which should offer better protection if the inflation dragon comes back. Similarly, the NEOS Enhanced Income 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (CSHI) allows to achieve an enhanced yield by having the downside risk tied to the U.S. T-bill risk. Finally, the NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI), which I have included in my portfolio, can shield the portfolio if small and mid cap equities remain flat or do not get as hurt as the AI related indices.

The bottom line

Most covered call ETFs do not bring any defensive to the table. So, if the SPY or QQQ plunge, the lion's share of ETF world would very likely follow. And if after the correction there is no immediate recovery, the chances would be quite high of experiencing structurally reduced passive income flows.

For most durable and retirement income investors (including myself), it is not an option.

The way to mitigate that is by tilting the covered call ETF portfolio towards specific vehicles that run their covered call overlay strategies on other underlying indices than SPY or QQQ.

As elaborated in the piece, a blend of IAUI, IYRI, CSHI and IWMI would, in my humble opinion, position income investors well for having their cash flows protected from a potential AI-driven sell-off.

data.title: IonQ Vs. Rigetti: The Quantum Pair Trade Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
In quantum computing, narratives and valuations make it difficult to call out absolute valuation-fundamentals gaps and time entries. I have favored QTUM in the past because it reduced idiosyncratic risks within the traded quantum stocks universe. However, a better risk-aware approach is a pair trade that should ideally ensure that relative fundamentals eventually play out and holding shorts becomes technically hedged to an extent, enabling longer-term positioning. Regardless of whether quantum stocks are in bubble territory or if narratives and potential continue to drive share prices further up, a well-established pair trade should make for an ideal setup.

IonQ, Inc. (IONQ) and Rigetti Computing, Inc. (RGTI) make for an ideal pair for many reasons. First, they are both fundamentally pre-economic, far from profitability and burning cash. Secondly, both firms rely heavily on government, defense, and academic ecosystems to subsidize their development. Lastly, both IonQ and Rigetti are pursuing universal gate-based quantum computing, not annealers or analog simulators (like D-Wave)—making them a natural pair trade within the same quantum sub-segment.

Fortunately, my thesis finds the relative fundamental valuation gap overwhelmingly in favor of IonQ, making the long IONQ and short RGTI a high-conviction setup. The gap is substantial, and the markets should see that too—hence investors entering into this setup should be able to have the appetite for a longer ride and remain invested if RGTI keeps testing the setup.

Completely Different Technology Momentum

IonQ has moved out of the lab and now claims 99.99% two-qubit fidelity. Fidelity determines how long a quantum circuit can run before noise overwhelms the computation. Every extra decimal point counts toward the higher ranges, and by a significant order of magnitude. At 99.99%, IonQ is now operating at a level where some practical advantage becomes possible in optimization and simulation tasks. According to management commentary in the Q3 2025 earnings call,

So our physical qubits are now substantially higher performance than anyone else's logical qubits. So in the short term, for the near-term road map, you can take our physical qubit count as you look at other people's logical qubit counts.

Rigetti, by contrast, is at 99.5% today and guiding to 99.7% in 2026 and 99.8% in 2027. That sounds close on paper, but is vastly different in practice. A move from 99.5% to 99.7% roughly doubles usable circuit depth; 99.7% to 99.8% is another doubling. But IonQ’s 99.99% gives another ~20x deeper circuit window, which translates directly into whether real applications (chemistry, complex optimization) run to completion.

This is because expected error grows roughly as "circuit depth * (1–fidelity)", the maximum usable depth scales as "1 ÷ (1–fidelity)". So 99.5% (error 0.005) allows ~200 layers, 99.7% (~0.003) allows ~330, and 99.8% (~0.002) allows ~500—but 99.99% (0.0001) jumps to ~10,000 layers, which is where real algorithms stop collapsing into noise. DARPA explicitly did not advance Rigetti to Stage B, stating they need improved plans for error correction and coupling — the two pillars of full-scale machines. IonQ, in contrast, did reach Stage B.

In terms of scalability, IonQ’s 2026 machine targets 256 qubits manufactured on mature semiconductor nodes. This enables supply chain resilience and lower costs as established foundries open up for manufacturing. Rigetti's roadmap requires increasing chip sizes and ultimately demands a new fab because their 150mm Fremont line cannot reach the uniformity required for ~99.9% fidelity. In other words, IonQ avoids this capex entirely by designing for standard manufacturing. Rigetti hits a capital wall as soon as it tries to cross into fault-tolerant territory.

Commercial Traction

IonQ raised guidance for FY2025 to $106-110m. Rigetti reported $1.9m for Q3 (down more than 20% YoY). IonQ's revenue scale is already at least 10x of Rigetti's, yet the market cap is only 2-2.5x of what Rigetti commands. It is probably simplifying the story too much simply comparing market caps with revenue numbers in early-stage companies, but there are nuances in revenue quality as well.

IonQ now has platform-based recurring revenues trickling in, national security contracts and multi-year system integration deals. Rigetti's revenues are primarily government and grant-driven at the moment. The biggest developments announced in Q3 were two 9-qubit system sales totaling $5.7m and a $5.8m AFRL contract recognized ratably over 3 years. Overall, IonQ's deployments are closer to real-life commercial workflows, while Rigetti is being used primarily for evaluation and R&D.

What Valuations Mean

IonQ has $3.5B in cash after its October raise and no debt. Rigetti has $600M, and no debt. However, runway needs appear to be vastly different. I feel IonQ has run its roadmap with lower capex requirements. Rigetti must spend hundreds of millions on a new fab or port its technology to a foundry. Despite the capital need differences, a net-of-cash value measure (simply cash as % of market cap) shows IonQ's cash reserves at 18% of the market cap. Rigetti's cash reserves (including equivalents) account for only ~6.5% of the market cap.

Strictly looking at the price-to-forward sales ratio, Rigetti is now trading at ~1200x levels compared to IonQ's less than 200x range. While the gap isn’t as simple as one stock being overvalued and the other cheap, it reflects two very different revenue bases and levels of market traction. But combining the valuations with the fundamental differences discussed, IonQ definitely seems to have a lower gap than Rigetti at today's levels.

The Pair Trade Implementation

The fundamentals-valuations gap is apparent. So what are we missing here if the pair trade of Short Rigetti and Long IonQ is as obvious as it is. The most difficult part of an IONQ-RGTI pair trade is not the fundamentals, but the path implications. Rigetti has a long history of insane rallies often 200-300%. IonQ does not move as much with more defined metrics to measure progress against, versus the narrative-based play in Rigetti.

This means being directionally right is not sufficient. Investors who identify the opportunity may still lose despite being right. The first thing we have to remove is shorting Rigetti outright—a 200-300% rally in a matter of a few weeks could challenge that setup. Quantum stocks often see borrow disappear during momentum spikes, triggering involuntary covering at the top. Also, the magnitude of moves is so different between the two stocks that the IonQ hedge may often not compensate enough—imagine a scenario when Rigetti is 200% up and IonQ is only up by 50%.

As implied volatility is very high, far OTM options with far expiries also trade at costs that may mean losses even if the underlying stock moves strongly in the right direction. A Rigetti $40 call expiring on the 23rd of January, 2026, is trading at ~4% of the current stock price. That implies Rigetti needs to move ~50% within a month or so to start hitting positional profits. Even if it tests $40, you may lose the option premium. This could add up over time. Such setups could be particularly harsh if both stocks consolidate for a long period while you bleed setup costs.

The best way to implement the pair trade is to size RGTI appropriately. Looking at the magnitude of price moves historically, $1 long IONQ for every $0.25–0.40 short RGTI looks like an appropriate balance. This ensures even a 300% move in RGTI drags the setup by ~75% to ~120%. IonQ’s expected long-duration compounding still offsets the shock, avoiding margin calls and the need to exit at peak mania phases. Other active measures such as altering position sizes, and reacting to trends, could also help allow IonQ to outrun Rigetti eventually, even if the trade takes a few months to a couple of years to play out. Combining positions with far OTM options as insurance could also work, depending on risk tolerances.

data.title: Record August World Oil Production
data.description:
A guest post by Ovi

The focus of this post is an overview of World oil production along with a more detailed review of the top 11 Non-OPEC oil-producing countries. OPEC production is covered in a separate post.

Below are a number of Crude plus Condensate (C + C) production charts, usually shortened to "oil," for the oil-producing countries. The charts are created from data provided by the EIA’s International Energy Statistics and are updated to August 2025. This is the latest and most detailed/complete World oil production information available. Information from other sources such as OPEC, IEA, STEO and country-specific sites such as Brazil, Norway, Mexico, Argentina and China is reported to provide a one- or two-month outlook.

The World’s August oil production increased by 405 kb/d to 85,244 kb/d, a new World Peak Oil All-Time High. Last month's projection for August’s production was 85,465 kb/d, too high by 221 kb/d.

This chart also projects World C + C production out to December 2026. It uses the December 2025 STEO report along with the International Energy Statistics to make the projection. Production in September is projected to increase by 1,439 kb/d to 86,683 kb/d; if correct, another new World Peak Oil next month. The large September C + C increase is due to the 1,165 kb/d increase in crude production shown in the crude graph. That increase then drops in December. The large drop is just as surprising as the increase.

The 12-month highest CMA shown at March 2026 is 85,547 kb/d vs. the September 2018 12-month CMA of 82,962 kb/d.

For December 2026, production is projected to be 85,519 b/d, a downward revision of 146 kb/d from last month.

August’s World oil output without the US increased by 318 kb/d to 71,450 kb/d. September’s production is expected to increase by 1,388 kb/d to 72,838 kb/d.

The projection is forecasting that December 2026 oil production will be 72,006 kb/d, an increase of 556 kb/d from August.

A Different Perspective on World Oil Production

August’s Big 3 oil production decreased by 24 kb/d to 32,917 kb/d. Saudi Arabia contributed a 100 kb/d drop to that decrease, while the US added 86 kb/d. While August’s drop was largely due to Saudi Arabia, OPEC’s increasing production, which started in April 2025, will continue up to December 2026 and will reverse this unexpected one-month drop.

Production in the remaining countries has been slowly increasing since the September 2020 low of 42,970 kb/d. Production in August 2025 rose by 429 kb/d to a new high of 52,327 kb/d.

Countries Expected to Grow Oil Production

This chart was first posted a number of months back and shows the combined oil production from five Non-OPEC countries, Argentina, Brazil, Canada, Guyana and the U.S., whose oil production is expected to grow. These five countries are often cited by OPEC and the IEA for being capable of meeting the increasing World oil demand for next year. For these five countries, production from April 2020 to July 2024 rose at an average rate of 1,130 kb/d/year, as shown by the orange OLS line.

To show the impact of US growth over the past 5 years, U.S. production was removed from the five countries, and that graph is shown in red. The production growth slope for the remaining four countries has been reduced by 614 kb/d/yr to 516 kb/d/yr.

August production has been added to the five growers chart, up by 439 kb/d to 24,449 kb/d. Of the 439 kb/d, Guyana added 250 kb/d, U.S. added 86 kb/d and Argentina added 44. For the Five growers W/O U.S., August production rose to 10,655 kb/d, up 146 kb/d from July.

Note: The OLS lines are updated to July.

World Oil Countries Ranked by Production

Above are listed the World’s 13th-largest oil-producing countries. In August 2025 these 13 countries produced 78.6% of the World’s oil. On a MoM basis, production decreased by 55 kb/d in these 13 countries, while on a YOY basis, production rose by 2,053 kb/d. Note the YoY increases from Brazil, Kazakhstan and U.S.

August Non-OPEC Oil Production Charts

August's Non-OPEC oil production increased by 452 kb/d to 55,179 kb/d. September is expected to add an additional 58 kb/d to 55,237 kb/d.

Using data from the December 2025 STEO, a projection for Non-OPEC oil output was made for the period August 2025 to December 2026 (Red graph). Output is expected to grow by 361 kb/d to reach 55,540 kb/d in December 2026.

August’s Non-OPEC W/O US oil production increased by 366 kb/d to 41,385 kb/d. September’s production is projected to add 7 kb/d to 41,392 kb/d.

From August 2025 to December 2026, production in Non-OPEC countries W/O the U.S. is expected to increase by 642 to 42,027 kb/d.

Non-OPEC Oil Countries Ranked by Production

Listed above are the World’s 11 largest Non-OPEC producers. The original criteria for inclusion in the table was that all of the countries produced more than 1,000 kb/d. Both Oman and Angola were below 1,000 kb/d for a few months but have rebounded to above 1,000 kb/d.

August’s MoM production decreased by 21 kb/d to 46,061 kb/d for these eleven Non-OPEC countries, while as a whole, the Non-OPEC countries saw a yearly production increase of 2,485 kb/d to 55,179 kb/d. Major yearly gains came from Brazil, Kazakhstan and U.S.

In August 2025, these 11 countries produced 83.5% of all Non-OPEC oil.

Non-OPEC Country’s Oil Production Charts

Angola’s August oil production dropped by 70 kb/d to 1,035 kb/d. I think the large drop is associated with the extra-large 200 kb/d increase reported last month by the EIA. According to the National Agency for Petroleum, Gas and Biofuels (NAP), July production was 999 kb/d. For August, the NAP reported production was 1,035 kb/d, same as the EIA.

According to the NAP, October production rose to 1,066 kb/d, red markers.

The EIA reported that Brazil’s August production decreased by 65 kb/d to 3,894 kb/d.

Brazil’s National Petroleum Association (BNPA) reported that production rose slightly in September and then rose by 116 kb/d in October to 4,025 kb/d, a new record high. The pre-salt blue graph tracks Brazil’s trend. Pre-salt production increased by 100 kb/d in October to 3,307 kb/d, also a new high.

Canada’s oil production decreased by 61 kb/d in August to 5,049 kb/d.

A projection has been made for September production based on preliminary production provided by the Canada Energy Regulator (CER). September’s projected production dropped to 5,024 kb/d. The CER estimate contains some NGLs, which have been removed from the projection. On average the EIA reduces the CER’s monthly production by 370 kb/d.

The EIA reported China’s August oil output rose by 30 kb/d to 4,300 kb/d.

The China National Bureau of Statistics reported September production rose to 4,325 kb/d and then in October dropped by 85 kb/d to 4,240 kb/d.

On a YoY basis, China’s August production increased by 101 kb/d from 4,199 kb/d.

According to the EIA, Kazakhstan’s August oil output increased by 65 kb/d to 2,147 kb/d.

Since Argus no longer reports OPEC+ crude production, production data for Kazakhstan will now be taken from the monthly IEA reports. In September 2025, pre-salt crude production rose to 1,920 kb/d, an upward revision of 80 kb/d from the IEA’s October report. In the IEA’s December report, they are reporting October’s production dropped by 230 kb/d to 1,690 kb/d and then rose to 1,800 kb/d in November.

The November oil production is 320 kb/d above the OPEC+ target set for Kazakhstan.

According to the EIA, Mexico’s August output dropped by 4 kb/d to 1,735 kb/d.

In June 2024, Pemex issued a new and modified oil production report for Heavy, Light and Extra Light oil. It is shown in blue in the chart, and it appears that Mexico is not reporting condensate production when compared to the EIA report.

In earlier EIA reports, they would add close to 55 kb/d of condensate to Pemex’s “Total Liquids” report. More recently, the EIA has been adding 90 kb/d of condensate to Mexican production. For September and October production, 90 kb/d have been added to the estimate. October’s production is estimated to be close to 1,731 kb/d. Note that Mexico’s production, as reported by them for the last four months, has stabilized around 1,650 kb/d, according to Pemex.

The EIA reported Norway’s August production dropped by 6 kb/d to 1,974 kb/d.

Separately, the Norway Petroleum Directorate (NPD) reported that October’s production dropped to 1,837 kb/d, red markers.

The Norway Petroleum Directorship also reported that October’s oil production was 0.4% above forecast.

According to the November OPEC MOMR: "Throughout the rest of the year, production is expected to be supported by the Johan Castberg and Jotun FPSOs, alongside robust volumes from Johan Sverdrup."

According to the EIA, August’s output rose by 5 kb/d to 1,008 kb/d and appears to have begun a budding growth phase. Previous production peaked in October 2022.

Qatar is now providing the EIA with monthly updated oil production back to January 2025. Qatar’s August output was reported to be 1,322 kb/d, up 8 kb/d from July.

The EIA reported Russia’s August 2025 C + C production decreased by 10 kb/d to 9,803 kb/d and was up 124 kb/d from August 2024.

Up to August 2025, Argus Media used to report OPEC+ crude production. That monthly report has now been discontinued. The above chart now also shows Russian production as reported by the IEA. It is difficult to assess the accuracy of the IEA report, but over the last few months the IEA’s Russian production has been around 100 kb/d to 150 kb/d higher than Argus Media's. The best that can be done at this time will be to compare the production trends between the EIA and the IEA. I think that Russian oil production continues to be a major state secret at this time because of the damage being caused by the heavy bombing to its crude oil processing-related facilities.

According to the IEA December report, October crude production was 9,240 kb/d, and then November production dropped by 210 kb/d to 9,030 kb/d. November production was 500 kb/d lower than the OPEC+ target set for Russia and is the first month that may be showing the effects of Ukrainian bombing.

This US production chart up to August 2025 is the same as the one published last week in the US update post. However, the projected portions of the two production graphs have been updated using the December 2025 STEO.

U.S. September projected production has been revised up by 74 kb/d to 13,869 kb/d.

Production in December 2026 is expected to be 13,513 kb/d, 92 kb/d lower than shown in last week’s US update. Production peaks in October 2025 and then begins a slow decline.

Note production in the Onshore L48 is essentially flat from July to December and begins to decline.

Oil production in Guyana has been flat since January 2024. However, for August 2025, production rose by 250 kb/d to a new high of 895 kb/d.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: QYLD: You Can Hold For The Income But It Won't Outperform It's Peers
data.description:
The covered-call ETF space continues to see an influx of products in addition to more capital being allocated toward them. The Fed just cut rates by 25 bps and Fed Chair Powell surprised many of us with his dovish pivot. Bond yields have started retracing and the United States 12-Month Bill (US12M) has seen its coupon decline to 3.56% while the United States 10-Year Bond (US10Y) has watched its coupon fall to 4.15%. We have a new Fed Chair starting in May and the market is currently pricing in at least 2 rate cuts in 2026. This is likely to make covered call products more attractive as the risk-free rate of return continues to fall. Investors won’t be able to hang out on the sidelines and manufacture meaningful income for much longer and a portion of that capital is likely to flow back into the capital markets.

The Global X Covered Call ETF (QYLD) is a pioneer in the income space but it’s seen better days where there weren’t as many strategies to choose from. The management team at Global X should look at the data that spans more than a decade and make changes to the investment rules because the current structure is allowing other investment houses to not only outperform QYLD’s returns but also gain market share. QYLD has just over $8 billion in AUM but this is a fund that is down -3.57% YTD where the Invesco QQQ ETF is up 22.37%. The large double-digit yield isn’t enough to make a compelling case as to why QYLD is a superior income producing asset in an environment where competing ETFs have generated larger YTD yields and appreciation. QYLD will continue to generate large amounts of income but until the management team reworks the mechanics I believe that they will be hard-pressed to capture the amount of inflows they would like to see.

Following up on my previous article about QYLD

In August I had written an article on QYLD (can be read here) where I had discussed why QYLD was losing its appeal. QYLD uses a single leg covered call strategy that has become outdated compared to other funds as its upside is impacting its ability to recover when shares fall. QYLD continues to underperform its peers which I feel is attributed to not updating their strategy. I am following up with a new article on QYLD as we have more data to make some projections with. I still believe that investors can do well from an income perspective if that is the ultimate goal with utilizing QYLD, but there is an opportunity for investors to look elsewhere for a higher yield and more appreciation.

QYLD has done a great job at generating income but it’s unable to outperform it’s peers

I have aggregated the data since QYLD’s inception and it has continuously produced a strong monthly distribution. QYLD went public at $25 back at the end of 2013 and since then has experienced an ongoing decline to $17.57. Since the end of 2022 QYLD has traded between $16-$20 and retraced to the $16 level several times. This is due to its structure of capping the upside with its call options. The big problem for QYLD is that it can do well in a flat or appreciating market but when we have big downturns like what happened in April it becomes difficult for shares to recover. The amount of income becomes discounted because its shares are likely in the red which is another reason why I haven’t been as bullish s I once was with QYLD.

Since its IPO QYLD has generated $27.41 of income through the distribution. It has never missed a single month and is on the verge of completing its 12th years of producing a monthly distribution. Long-term investors have benefited from the income but at a huge sacrifice on capital appreciation. Since inception shares of QYLD are down -29.72% as its share price has declined from $25 to $17.57. The distribution yield since inception is 109.65% which places the annualized yield just over 9%. Long-term investors have a total ROI of $19.98 per share when the negative appreciation and income are combined for a total return of 79.935. If you don’t care about the underlying investment then QYLD does produce a reliable distribution that is quite large but these statistics are not bullish and there are alternatives that are more lucrative.

I compared QYLD to the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) as my baselines for the market. Next I constructed the peer group of the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO). and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ). The results from a YTD perspective are not bullish and do not paint a great picture for QYLD. On the baseline QQQ and SPY established a total ROI range of 18.49% - 22.76%. I don’t expect a covered call ETF to match the market but the peer group are all focused on the Nasdaq 100 and there are many alternatives that have generated more income and a larger total return.

On a YTD basis QYLD has delivered -3.57% in capital appreciation and 10.21% in distribution yield for a total return of 6.64%. When QQQ is up 22.76% this is hard to look past and when some investors will look for alternatives. JEPQ has a total return over this period of 14.47% consisting of 4.63% of appreciation and 9.84% distribution yield. GPIQ has been a solid fund with a bit more yield at 10.58% and 8.89% of appreciation for a total return of 10.58%. QDVO has outpaced QYLD in both areas with 11.33% of distribution yield and 9.55% of appreciation for a total return of 20.88%. QQQI has generated the largest yield with 14.18% in addition to appreciating by 5.06% for a total return of 19.24%. When I look at the data its hard to accept the fact that QYLD has fallen so far behind. The main reason why I can’t be bullish is that I can accomplish my initial goal of generating income without sacrificing all the upside with ETFs from its competitors.

How QYLD is constructed and what I think they should do to optimize its performance

QYLD is an equity income ETF designed to convert a meaningful portion of the NASDAQ-100’s price volatility into monthly cash flow through its distribution. QYLD holds a portfolio of equities that tracks the Nasdaq 100 and then systematically sells call options against that exposure. It’s objective is to deliver a higher level of distributable income by monetizing option premiums which comes at the expense of some upside participation when markets rally. The way that I would look at QYLD is that it builds exposure to the Nasdaq then sells a way most if not all the following month’s upside in exchange for option premium to be paid out in the distribution. The strategy works to a degree but sacrificing all of the upside has proven that it leaves the shareholders at a large disadvantage. QYLD has amazed more than $8 billion in AUM and I don’t think that its call strategy is appealing enough to keep the AUM increasing due to how the call side of the construction works.

QYLD’s covered call overly strategy allows QYLD to sell call options on the NASDAQ-100 using an index option approach which is implemented through NDX options. QYLD is basically agreeing to sell the index at a preset strike price if the index rises above that level before expiration. The call-writing is typically done in a rules-based way and re-established on a regular schedule. The big problem is that when the market falls, the option premium provides some offset, but it typically does not fully protect against sustained equity drawdowns. When the market falls quickly over a 1-2 month period QYLD is forced to write a new set of call options to generate income and is likely to get caught in a situation where a rally has occurred while their shares are capped. This is why QYLD never seems to be able to fully recover. I think that QYLD should implement a call spread component where it retains part of the premium generated from writing covered calls and use that to purchase naked out of the money calls to generate some upside in the event the market rises. This is how similar funds are able to protect the equity in the portfolio.

Risks to investing in QYLD

While I am neutral on QYLD there are still risk factors for investors to consider. QYLD’s biggest structural risk is that it systematically sells call options on the NASDAQ-100 which caps the upside. This is an intentional trade off as the option premium is the lions share of the cash distribution. Due to the option strategy QYLD has tremendous downside risk as its not able to recover after the markets take a sharp decline. For investors that are relying on the income the fact that the distribution is constructed from option premium means that it will fluctuate and it could decline significantly in the future if there is less volatility in the market. Investors should do their due diligence and make sure that QYLD fits their investment needs.

Conclusion

The reason why I am neutral on QYLD is because the recurring income continues to generate double digit yields. If it wasn’t for the consistently of the distribution I would turn bearish as the mechanics of the strategy has left long-term shareholders in a situation where QYLD has been unable to get back to a level that at least puts their initial investment at even money. There are too many alternatives to QYLD and I have been allocating capital toward QQQI and GPIQ over QYLD. What would cause me to exit the position is if Global X decides not to update QYLD’s mechanics or if the characteristics of the distribution deteriorate. I think that QYLD will continue to generate a large amount of income but don’t expect the underlying share price to do much especially if we get an elongated retracement in the market.

data.title: Air Products And Chemicals: De-Risking Moves Support A Re-Rating
data.description:
We are revisiting Air Products and Chemicals (NYSE:APD) following the release of its Q3 results (the company’s fiscal fourth quarter 2025) and the announcement of advanced negotiations on potential partnerships for low-emission ammonia projects. APD remains a difficult name to assess, and since our previous update, the stock has slipped roughly 16% (Fig. 1). It is also not the first time we have reported this trade-off between being the first mover in the hydrogen economy and remaining supported. For our new readers, the company provides specialty gases to manufacturers, energy players, and healthcare facilities.

Fig 1

FY 2025 Results

We had flagged the risk of elevated restructuring costs and a one-off hit under the new CEO, and this has now materialised. In number, Q4 results include approximately $795 million in pre-tax charges related to business and asset actions ($3.33 per share). EPS was effectively flat/near zero. Last year, the company also benefited from a gain on the sale of its LNG business. Still, in a challenging year, the company's results were solid; sales and adjusted EPS declined both by 3% (compared to 2024 - Fig. 2).

Source: APD Fiscal Year 2025 results - Fig 2

Why are we Positive?

On the Louisiana low-carbon ammonia project (2.8 million tons), APD noted that discussions with Yara continue to move forward (Fig. 3). The two sides are working toward an arrangement under which Yara would contribute roughly 25% of the project’s capital cost and take 100% of the ammonia offtake. A similar commercial logic applies to NEOM, where Yara would receive a marketing commission for placing volumes that APD cannot sell directly. Securing an offtake partner is clearly constructive for APD, though the company still faces roughly six more months before reaching a final investment decision. APD maintains that returns on its share of the project remain compelling, potentially even above the group average, although it will take time to demonstrate this. Total project capex has now been revised upward to $8–9 billion, compared with the previous ~$8 billion estimate. Under the current framework, Yara would allocate about 25% of total capex to ammonia units, storage, and logistics assets, implying a contribution of about $2.12 billion at the midpoint. One advantage for APD in this structure is that it retains ownership of the hydrogen units, which the company believes should generate average to above-average returns, supported in part by available tax credits. At the midpoint, APD’s net investment would come to roughly $6.37 billion, of which $2 billion has already been deployed. Depending on production levels, Air Products and Chemicals' annual tax credit could reach $425 million at 5 million tons (and double that at 10 million tons). Carbon-capture capability gives the company additional bargaining power when negotiating commercial terms with potential ammonia buyers, as it directly enhances the economics.

Fig 3

Turning to NEOM (1.2 million tons of green ammonia), the company still does not have a fixed-price offtake agreement, which we view as a drawback. This is negative, leaving APD exposed to ammonia market volatility; however, bringing Yara in as a partner helps limit downstream capital exposure. The project is now over 90% complete and is expected to begin operating in 2027. APD currently holds rights to up to 1.2 million tons of green ammonia per year. The company plans to enter a marketing and distribution agreement with Yara for unsold volumes, particularly those not used for green-hydrogen applications in Europe. In parallel, APD still has a long-term contract with Total, covering roughly 40% of the offtake beginning in 2030. The agreement will effectively cover all volumes from 2027 until TotalEnergies’ contract starts, and is expected to be finalized in H1 2026. Given volatile prices, this might put the company under margin pressure (and, given its earnings sensitivity to Wall Street consensus, it might pressure APD shares down). That said, this structure reduces the company’s commercialization risk.

Looking at the latest quarter and ADP's 2026 guidance (Fig. 4), we expect the market to react positively going forward. Firstly, the ADP outlook came in ahead of consensus. Still, upside came from the Corporate segment, which more than compensated for softer results in the Americas and Asia. Moreover, the CAPEX plan was reduced by $1 billion (this will ease Wall Street analysts' D&A projections), and more importantly, APD sees a +7–9% EPS growth target.

Fig 4

Earnings Change and Valuation

Assessing the company's finances is not easy. 2026 CAPEX is now guided to ~$4 billion, with an updated slide in the deck indicating ~$3 billion in 2027 spend. This is positive. Taking advantage of the APD EPS growth trajectory, the company should increase earnings by 8% to $13 (midpoint of the outlook - Fig. 4). Two key factors behind the earnings growth opportunity are also a $100 million benefit from employee reductions and lower equipment sales cost overruns for an additional $100 million. In our previous assessment, we forecasted an EPS growth of only 5%. While one could question our target P/E for APD, we are keeping it at 25x, reflecting the company’s resilient performance in a challenging year and the improving EPS growth profile, as key risks are progressively reduced. Our P/E target is also very much aligned with APD's 5-year historical valuation. Thus, we set a price target of $325 per share, representing 34% upside.

Risks

We had long covered APD's downside risks, so we report lower results relative to peers and the company's CEO succession. The company is exposed to CAPEX inflation and potential additional restructuring costs. Additionally, being present in Asia and the EU, APD is impacted by FX and lower-than-anticipated JV income. Roughly 60% of Air Products’ backlog relates to projects being developed without a committed anchor customer, with pricing only determined later and no precise mechanism for capital cost pass-through. Last time, we also reported US limited property ownership as another potential risk.

Conclusion

All in all, we remain buyers. With the Yara agreements, part of the APD assets are de-risked. The company provided a positive EPS outlook for next year (with a core operating profit margin >30%) and deserves a supportive valuation.

data.title: Fastly: SaaS Play With Breakout Potential In 2026
data.description:
Content delivery network provider Fastly, Inc. (FSLY) reported better-than-expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter in November that beat on both the bottom and the top lines. As a result, due to stronger growth, shares of Fastly have seen a breakout to the upside in November, but have lost some momentum again lately.

Fastly is on track to be free cash flow profitable on a full-year basis, and the SaaS platform raised its FY 2025 revenue outlook amid strong traction in its core enterprise business. The company is also seeing solid RPO growth and booked its third consecutive quarter of free cash flow profitability. I like the firm’s value proposition and see accelerating growth momentum in 2026.

Previous rating

Fastly was a Strong Buy for me due to the Saas platform having a ton of customer acquisition momentum and doing well in the enterprise segment of the market: Improving Investment Setup. In the most recent quarter, Fastly improved its net retention sequentially by 2 PP and maintained a high 94% share of enterprise customers in its portfolio. Fastly could be on track to new revenue records in the 2026 fiscal year, and I see shares of the SaaS enterprise as undervalued given its improving profitability profile as well as high single-digit growth in performance obligations.

Strong RPO backdrop, margin momentum

Fastly generated $158.2M in revenues in the third fiscal quarter, showing 15% year-over-year growth, with security-related revenues surging 30% Y/Y. This growth was in part driven by a higher number of enterprise customers in the SaaS company’s portfolio as well as higher net retention rates. In the third-quarter, Fastly had 627 enterprise customers, showing 9% year-over-year growth. The firm’s total enterprise revenue share grew 2 PP Y/Y to 94%. Retention, on a last trailing-twelve months basis was 106%, showing 2 PP Q/Q expansion.

Besides double-digit top line growth, Fastly maintained its momentum in RPO growth. Remaining performance obligations are a key measure for SaaS platforms that sign multi-year service contracts and therefore recognize revenue over time as their products and services are consumed. In the most recent quarter, Fastly's RPO surged 9% Q/Q to $315.1M.

Fasting has seen a considerable improvement in its free cash flow profile in the last year as well, which is one reason why shares are up 55% since I last wrote about them. In the third fiscal quarter, SaaS company achieved $18.1M free cash flow, showing a year-over-year swing of a massive $25.2M. The enterprise is therefore on track to have its first full-year of positive free cash flows in FY 2025… which is a major milestone achievement for a company that consistently lost money pretty much throughout its history.

In terms of EBITDA, Fastly posted its second consecutive quarter of strong earnings growth: in Q3 '25 the platform revealed $25.7M in adjusted EBITDA, showing 189% quarter-over-quarter growth.

Compared to other content network delivery service firms, most notably Cloudflare (NET), Fastly is much smaller in terms of both revenue and gross profits. But Fastly is growing rapidly in all major metrics and seeing considerable momentum on a gross profit basis as well.

In the most recent quarter, Fastly generated a gross profit of $99.3M, showing 46% growth over the last three years. While under-performing Cloudflare in terms of gross profit growth, Fastly has a ton of margin momentum right now which is due to the fact that the SaaS platform finally reached positive margin territory.

Breakout potential if Fastly executes on growth

Fastly is currently valued at a price-to-revenue ratio of 2.3X which compares against a forward P/S ratio of 26.6X for Cloudflare, a major competitor in the CDN field for Fastly, and 2.8X for Akamai Technologies (AKAM).

Shares of Fastly are trading about 11% below the firm's 3-year average price-to-revenue ratio while the industry group P/S ratio sits at 10.6X. Cloudflare is trading at a significant premium to Fastly because the company is achieving significantly stronger growth in its core market of providing content delivery network services: Cloudflare is expected to grow its top line at least 23% each year in the next five years.

For comparison, Fastly is expected to grow its revenues at an average rate of less than 6% in the next four years. This growth differential is reflected in the valuations of both Fastly and Cloudflare. However, on a positive note, Fastly guided for $610-614M in FY 2025 compared to $594-602M previously, showing a 2% raise at the mid-point.

Considering that Fastly is making massive progress by improving its EBITDA and free cash flow trajectory, not to mention its gross profits, I believe that the SaaS company is undervalued. In my last work, I proposed that Fastly could trade at least at the 3-year average P/S ratio which yielded a fair value calculation of $11.70/share at the time. My fair value based off of the current 3-year average P/S ratio of 2.6X is $11.76/share, highlighting about 12% upside revaluation potential for the company's shares.

Risks with Fastly

Fastly's biggest risk, as I see it, relates to the company issuing a lot of stock to its senior managers. In the third-quarter, Fastly spent $33.1M on stock-based compensation, or SBC, which calculates to a revenue share of 21% in Q3 '25 compared to 18% in the year-earlier period. High stock-based compensation is a risk for investors because of the associated dilution risk as well as potential mis-alignment of incentives between senior managers and shareholders.

Final thoughts

The SaaS platform reported stronger-than-expected earnings in the third-quarter which caused investors to bid up Fastly's price and push shares into a new up-leg. The main reason for this change in investor perception was that Fastly has been able to accelerate its growth in its core enterprise market while improving its profitability on a number of metrics (EBITDA, gross profits and free cash flow).

Fastly achieved its third consecutive quarter of free cash flow profitability in Q3 '25. Gross profit momentum and strong enterprise customer counts support an investment in Fastly in 2025, and so does the solid development of the company's RPO. I like Fastly as an underdog in the SaaS market and see shares as undervalued. If Fastly can sustain its enterprise segment growth, maintain high gross profit margins (or expand them) and increase its RPO consistently throughout 2026, I could see a much higher fair value than $11.70/share in the long term.

data.title: Realty Income Is Still Looking For New Ways To Grow (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Today, we circle back to net lease real estate, the bread and butter of REITer's Digest. Over the past several months, I've published a variety of articles discussing net lease real estate from various perspectives. Recently, I published an article on the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), where I discussed the impacts of stabilizing net lease capitalization rates. More recently, I published an article on Agree Realty (ADC), outlining the company's high-quality portfolio and emphasis on creditworthy tenants. With an uncertain macroeconomic backdrop, a high quality portfolio appears to be earning its stripes.

Just last month, I published an article covering Realty Income (O), the blue chip of net lease real estate. One of the oldest publicly traded REITs, and the largest in net lease by a mile, the big O is a behemoth in net lease real estate.

I mentioned this in nearly every particle that I publish on O, the company owns more properties than many of its competitors combined. O recently announced another incremental increase to the company's monthly dividend finally reaching the mark of $0.27 per share per month. With another dividend increase in the bank, let's dive back into O and review prior coverage.

Review of Prior Coverage

Last month, I published an article covering O's Q3 earnings release. The article was centered around finding value in the net lease sector. Beyond analyzing the high points of the company's Q3 earnings report, we dove into the company's dividend yield spread against the 10 year treasury.

Following a period of prolonged compression, I noted that O's yield spread against the risk free rate had begun to expand since the beginning of November.

As it stood more than one month ago, the company's dividend yield was finally paying more than 100 basis points more than the 10 year treasury. Today, the company continues to be priced at a similar premium.As the behemoth company continued to chart its growth trajectory billions of dollars of real estate every three months, I continue to emphasize the significant change going on at the monthly dividend company.

A key point across my prior coverage of the company has been O's change from a net lease landlord that bought Taco Bell restaurants and childcare facilities to the biggest on the block. Over time, the company's business has changed almost completely. Today, the company is hardly the net lease REIT that we knew over the past 50 years. Nowadays, the company owns a gigantic international portfolio that also includes data centers, casinos, and cold storage facilities.

For more on that, I suggest checking out my article, O, How You've Changed.

What's New At O?

Since the publication of my prior article discussing the rapid changes being made to O's business model, the company has continued to make changes to their platform. Over the past year, there have been several new changes to the company, many of which are significant. Let's dive in and get specific.

Over the past year, there's been a relatively quiet hubbub around O regarding their venture into the private spheres. Note that 'spheres' is in fact plural there. Across coverage of O and in the comments, I see misconceptions around their ventures into both private capital and private credit.

The important distinction is that their new private capital business includes a new perpetual life fund that will invest in net lease assets for a management fee. Meanwhile, private credit means that O has actually begun lending to tenants rather than just leasing the real estate as their traditional business entails.

Neither of these are straight down O's historical fairway.

CEO Sumit Roy commented on adding a perpetual life fund to O's busy business.

As we establish ourselves in the private capital arena, our long track record of producing equity-like total returns with bond-like stability is resonating with investors. We recently launched a perpetual life fund and expect that initiative will provide additional capital to support our growth objectives and enhance our liquidity position.

Managing private capital means that the company is now responsible for separating assets into either the O bucket or the O's clients bucket, creating a difficult decision that must be made by the company's management team. In the past, we've seen other businesses try similar moves to drive scalable revenue from a platform when their cost of capital is no longer permissive. There's some overlapping similarity W.P. Carey's (WPC) failed venture into the TPAM space.

Going one step further, it seems even stranger that O would begin making loans to their tenants beyond signing long term leases. CEO Sumit Roy described that making a loan to a tenant is not so different from a sale leaseback of 10 or 20 years.

Bennett RoseCitigroup Inc., Research Division

I wanted to ask you a little bit about the loans you made in Europe in the quarter. It looks like you found some very healthy opportunities on the yield side. Can you share maybe who those were with and if you would expect to see significant opportunity in that kind of arena over the next quarters or so?

Sumit RoyPresident, CEO & Director

So Smedes, as we've said, when we started going down the credit investment path of one of the verticals that we wanted to sort of lean into, there was a strategic rationale behind it. We were -- what we had shared with you was we were already long the clients. We were doing 15-year, 20-year sale leasebacks with these clients. And we were long the credit. We were very comfortable the credit.

In both cases, you're locking up the capital as a commitment based on the clients' financials. However, it seems strange that O, a real estate company, has decided to foray into banking ventures where they're making capital contributions directly to the balance sheet of a tenant. Usually, a company like O would want to leverage their real estate expertise to use their capital to buy real estate, rather than provide private loans to existing tenants. Perhaps in a downside, O could loan a tenant money to pay the rent they are owed?

This type of investment also aligns with their recent deal with Blackstone (BX) to purchase an $800 million slice of preferred equity from City Center in Las Vegas.

This is an interesting deal that roughly aligns with O's past preferred equity deals with BX. In this case, the company has made an $800 million perpetual loan backed by the real estate at City Center. Details of the deal can be found below including transaction metrics.

The perpetual preferred equity investment is expected to carry an initial unlevered rate of return to Realty Income (O) of 7.4% with annual, capped escalators starting on the fifth anniversary of the closing. What's more, the investment is subject to an early redemption premium payable to Realty Income...

From my perspective, this points towards an interesting trend for O. Over the years, I've talked about the company encountering challenges around their supersized growth over the past decade. By acquiring more than 10,000 properties as well as competing REITs, O has found themselves in a precarious position of needing to deploy enormous amounts of capital in a scalable format. Nontraditional deals like lending to tenants or buying an $800 million preferred slice out of City Center are opportunities for a company like O to deploy capital as fast as they need to continue to grow the business.

However, from the perspective of a shareholder, many of these deals appear different from O's historical path. With that in mind, each of these investments is absorbed into the existing shares that are held by O's investors. I look at this as an overall dilution of the quality of my investment where a portfolio of high quality net lease assets has now been turned into an internationally diversified portfolio of general investments.

Conclusion

O is trying to grow. Looking at the company's lofty guidance, the company needs to deploy billions of dollars of capital every quarter to meet investor expectations. From my personal experience in the net lease sector, it is difficult to acquire that volume of assets without allowing your quality parameters to begin to slip. In the case of O, the company has moved towards a model of deploying capital at scale, rather than looking for incremental investment opportunities like ADC.

I see this as a significant long-term risk to the health of O's business. They venture further and further from their traditional business means investors' shares are backed by a constantly evolving portfolio of assets. I would strongly prefer disciplined growth backed by prudent capital recycling. With that, O earns a Hold rating.

data.title: McCormick: A Flavor Utility Paying A Policy Surcharge
data.description:
McCormick’s flavor franchise remains healthy and in a phased restart in volume-driven growth even as it faces tariffs and an expansion in Mexico. On current stock prices, this company appears to be a high-quality dividend-growing compounder temporarily stymied by policy, rather than a broken franchise.

Business Quality: Flavor as Everyday Infrastructure

McCormick & Company (MKC) has evolved from a local spice vendor, established in 1889, to the current worldwide leader in herbs, spices, seasoning blends, and sauces, with a product presence in around 150 different countries. The company operates with a twofold business model that in essence has two closely integrated engines. The Consumer segment, which contributes a bit over half of total sales, markets branded spices, seasoning mixes, hot sauces, mustards, and condiments to consumer markets, including grocery, mass, and e-commerce, through its brands like McCormick, Old Bay, Lawry’s, Schwartz, Ducros, Frank’s RedHot, French’s, and Cholula.

The Flavor Solutions business, which approaches forty percent of sales, is upstream. This business develops customized seasoning solutions, marinades, coatings, and flavorings for packaged food companies and restaurant chains, where this seasoning solution becomes part of their own recipe. Replacing this solution in their system involves not merely a pricing component but also flavor, supply chain considerations, labeling, and in some cases, regulatory reform. There are costs of switching that a commodity company does not incur.

What sets McCormick apart structurally is that it has control over the flavor stack from soup to nuts. McCormick sources whole world supplies of herbs and spices, has a robust sensory and R&D platform, and has assets like their long-running Flavor Forecast initiative that taps into early trends in flavor. This data for both their consumer side of their business and their B2B side, which creates a closed loop where data from consumers and chef influences becomes new product formulations for foodservice and consumer product companies, which then creates volume in their institutions that then creates economies of scale in their sourcing and manufacturing that drives their shelf business. This has a low-visibility role in building new innovations, where a company like a snack company that needs a new product in chili-lime powder, a quick-service chain that wants a different fried chicken seasoning, where McCormick has all their own knowledge of the application of those types of goods.

Hot sauce and condiments are a perfect example of this moat. The purchase of Frank’s RedHot and French’s in 2017, followed by Cholula in 2020, pushed McCormick even further into spaces where brand loyalty is very high and usage per serving is infinitesimal. Trading down from a favorite hot sauce brand or a favorite mayonnaise brand merely to cut costs costs precious little in a grocery basket but translates to a sharp degradation of quality; as such, sensitivity to pricing has low elasticity. McCormick’s plan to consolidate McCormick de México, where their McCormick Mayonesa brand is a leading brand in that category, further integrates this multi-channel, multi-brand heat and condiment offering.

Positioning for Uncertainty

The environment for packaged food companies has moved from straight-cost inflation to a complicated balance of consumer normalizing and trade policy. McCormick needed to demonstrate that their model is robust for both. Following intense pricing in 2022-2023, FY 2024 was a repair year, with net sales increasing around 1% to around $6.7 billion, with organic sales also increasing 1%, led by modest pricing and a small positive volume/mix contribution. Consumer sales increased around 1% to around $3.9 billion, with Flavor Solutions sales also increasing around 1% to around $2.9 billion, despite being impacted by a small divestiture in their canning business.

Gross profit margin improvement of around 90 basis points to around 38 1/2% of sales reflected product mix, past-year pricing initiatives, as well as benefits from Comprehensive Continuous Improvement and Global Operating Effectiveness initiatives. Operating income did grow to a bit over $1.0 billion, with earnings per share rising from around $2.52 to a bit over $2.92. On an adjusted basis, EPS of around $2.95 marked a near 9% improvement from a year ago. This, of course, does not count as hyper-growth, but it indicates that the engine is beginning to repair itself from that inflation shock.

Management’s approach for the rest of 2025 has been to re-emphasize volume. So, their goal has been differentiated volume-led growth, not a series of further broader-based price rises, and this approach has now shown up in the trend, as McCormick has reported several quarters of volume-led organic sales growth. In third-quarter 2025, net sales were around 3% higher, with total sales of about $1.7 billion, organic sales of about 2%, with volume and mix driving over a point of that. This is a very positive indicator that, for a product in a mature category, their brands and innovation are doing their job.

This resilience is rooted in their categories. When inflation affects households, consumers will want to trade down their protein sources and starches, but they will still want spices and hot sauce, which are relatively cheap ways of upgrading less expensive core ingredients. This so-called small luxury attribute provides a resilience benefit to McCormick’s portfolios that many center store categories lack.

There is, however, a new stress in tariffs. A general import tariff in the U.S. and a special surcharge for goods imported from China are considerations for a company like McCormick, which sources crops such as pepper, cinnamon, and vanilla from their origins. While gross tariffs are now seen as tens of millions of dollars in this year, with total gross annualized tariffs about twice that amount. For a company with over $1 billion in operating income, this represents a significant policy tax. McCormick is, in fact, not passing this total amount on in total in terms of pricing. The company takes this amount in exchange for protecting volume trends, as well as their brands. On the other hand, the company is not merely playing defense. Indeed, the agreement to acquire an additional 25% stake in McCormick de México from long-standing partner Grupo Herdez, in which it would take ownership to 75%, seems a shrewd move to further capitalize on a structurally growing market and secure a position in condiments and sauces. The JV provides around $800 million in net sales, with sales in mid-single digits, with a strong franchise in mayonnaise and other condiments. Post-acquisition, it would increase condiments and sauces to around one-fifth of McCormick’s worldwide sales, and then emerge as a sales, margin, and adjusted EPS accretive transaction in the first full year of acquisition. This is classic McCormick, where it seeks to capitalize on a world of access where their fundamental flavor platform would work and where private label would be structurally challenged.

Financials: What the Numbers Reveal

Compared to the 2024 base and 2025 run rate, McCormick appears to be a consistent but not exciting compounder with earnings being temporarily impacted by tariffs. Net sales for 2024 did increase slightly to $6.7 billion, with organic sales increasing 1%. Revenue for the Consumer Segment was $3.9 billion, with Flavor Solutions selling $2.9 billion, also increasing 1% in both cases. Gross margin did increase around a percentage point due to a favorable mix, pricing, and cost savings, with adjusted operating income growing in the low digits to over $1.0 billion.

Operating cash flow was just below $1.0 billion, with capex in the mid-$200 millions, leaving free cash flow in the mid-$600 millions, or high single-digit multiples of sales. The company's balance sheet finished 2024 with net leverage in low- to mid-3x EBITDA, which, while rich for a staple, was not egregious in a company with this amount of stability in cash flow and this long history of access to credit markets.

The cadence over the period through 2025 has now reinforced both the positive trends and the challenges. In 1Q 2025, net sales were approximating $1.6 billion, effectively unchanged from the previous year as 2% volume and mix were mitigated by adverse pricing. Adjusted EPS was just above $0.60, a slight decrement from the previous year and below market expectations, as revenue investments and early tariffs dampened margins. In the second quarter, net sales were up to $1.66 billion, with organic sales of 2% and adjusted EPS comparatively flat from a year ago but ahead of expectations, owing to working in efficiency. The third quarter then marked a volume-driven period, with net sales of 3% increase, organic sales of 2%, and adjusted EPS of around $0.85, which was slightly above market expectations, despite a sharp gross margin decrement of over a full percentage point due to tariffs as well as commodity-driven pressures.

The aggregate impact from this outlook perspective is reflected in guidance. Following the 2024 print, management has guided 2025 adjusted EPS in a range of a little above $3.00 per share for low single-digit net sales growth. Following the third-quarter 2025 earnings and updated estimates of tariffs, this EPS range has been reduced by a small amount, but sales continue to be flat to low single-digit growth in reported sales and low single digits in constant currency. This means that the fundamental driver of demand and mix is about where management thought it would be, but a policy surcharge and a few commodities are costing a few cents per share.

Valuation: What Are You Paying for Quality?

At a recent share price in the mid-$60s, McCormick now trades around 22 times trailing results, 20 times current-year adjusted EPS guidance, with a multiple in the mid-teens for EV/EBITDA, and just above three times for EV/Sales. This, of course, remains a premium compared with mainstream packaged food companies like General Mills and Kraft Heinz, which can maintain high-single-digit to low-teens multiples for P/E ratios, as well as low-digit multiples for EV/EBITDA ratios, but represents a sharp discount from McCormick’s former valuation, where it has traded in a range around a high 20s multiple for P/E ratios and a high teens ratio for EV/EBITDA.

From a cash flow perspective, free cash flow in the mid-$600 millions seems to warrant a high single-digit FCF yield on EV and a mid-single-digit yield on equity. With low single-digit sales growth, additional gross-margin expansion in the low single digits as a rough estimate of further improvement, excluding the hit from tariffs, and a return of capex to a normal run rate of a quarter billion per year, a free cash flow in the $700-800 range over the next few years seems surprisingly conservative. On a simplistic discounted cash flow model with 2-3% organic growth in these cash flows, using a mid-single-digit Cyr, a reasonable equity value would range from around low $70s. This would imply a wide range of percentage gains above current market prices, as well as a dividend yield in the high 2% range.

Rather, it is a question of whether a multiple of 20 for a flavor utility with a storied dividend growth story, limited cyclicality, and a temporary tariff overhang, compared with other places where one can deploy funds with a shot at low double-digit returns, let alone mid-single-digit returns, is all that compelling. To my mind, it’s a discount for policy risk, but not a discount due to a lack of faith in the business.
